question: Do scale-free network properties (power-law degree distributions) emerge in MCP tool federation graphs, where repos are nodes and shared tools/protocols are edges?
hypothesis: In a multi-repo AI agent ecosystem, tool connectivity follows a power-law distribution — a few hub repositories (like governance repos) concentrate disproportionately many tool connections, while most repos have few. This mirrors scale-free networks in software dependency graphs, the web, and biological networks.
evidence: Software dependency graphs (npm, PyPI, crates.io) consistently show power-law degree distributions — a few packages are depended on by thousands while most have few dependents
Preferential attachment (new tools more likely to connect to already well-connected repos) is the standard mechanism for scale-free emergence and applies to tool ecosystems
In the Demerzel ecosystem, the governance repo (Demerzel) connects to all consumer repos (ix, tars, ga) while consumer repos connect to fewer peers — consistent with hub-and-spoke topology
Governance structures and deliberate architecture may dampen pure preferential attachment — controlled ecosystems may show truncated power laws rather than pure scale-free behavior
Cross-validated with GPT-4o-mini: medium agreement — strong theoretical and empirical precedent from related domains, but specific MCP federation data needed for confirmation
